coredata.dc:title: A highly sensitive and selective detection of Cr(VI) and ascorbic acid based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yuhua
Fang, Xian
Zhao, Hong
Li, Zengxi
coredata.dc:description: A highly sensitive and selective detection of hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) and ascorbic acid (AA) was proposed using nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs). In the absence of AA, the quantitative detection of Cr(VI) was realized through Cr(VI) acting as a quencher to quench the fluorescence of N-CDs by inner filter effect (IFE) and static quenching effect. Under the optimal conditions, the linear range for Cr(VI) detection was from 0.01 to 250μM with a detection limit of 5nM (S/N = 3). In the presence of AA, the fluorescence intensity could be rapidly enhanced compared with the fluorescence of N-CDs/Cr(VI) system since Cr(VI) can be reduced into trivalent chromium (Cr(III)) by AA. And a wide linear range for AA detection was obtained from 1 to 750μM. The detection limit was 0.3μM (S/N = 3). More importantly, this method can be successfully applied to the detection of Cr(VI) in real water samples, and AA in vitamins C tablets and human serum sample.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-CDs
Cr(VI)
Ascorbic acid
Inner filter effect
Static quenching effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence determination of Cr(VI) and AA
Principle study of Cr(VI) and AA sensing strategy
Fluorescence determination of AA based on redox reaction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) FT-IR spectra; (B) Survey XPS spectra; (C) High resolution C 1s spectra of N-CDs and (D) TEM image of N-CDs.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs (red line) and N-CDs (blue line). The inset shows the images of the N-CDs solution taken under visible light (a) and 365nm UV lamp (b). (B) Fluorescence spectra of CDs (red line) and N-CDs (blue line) with the maximum excitation wavelength at 360nm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of Cr(VI) (olive line) and fluorescence excitation (red line) and emission (blue line) spectra of N-CDs; (B) Time-resolved fluorescence decay curves of N-CDs in the absence and presence of different concentrations of Cr(VI); (C) UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDs/Cr(VI) (500μM) in the presence of different concentrations of AA; (D) The amplified absorption spectra of (C) from 500 to 700nm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence responses of N-CDs upon addition of various concentrations of Cr(VI) (from top to bottom, 0, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 5, 10, 25, 50, 75, 100, 150, 250, 400, 500, 750 and 1000μM); (B) Linear relationship between I 0/I and Cr(VI) concentration in the range from 0.01 to 250μM (I and I 0 are the fluorescence intensities of N-CDs at 448nm in the presence and absence of Cr(VI), respectively); (C) Interference of different metal ions on the determination of Cr(VI); (D) Interference of various anions on the detection of Cr(VI). Cr(VI): 100μM, other ions: 200μM.

(A) Fluorescence responses of N-CDs/Cr(VI) system upon addition of various concentrations of AA (from bottom to top, 0, 1, 5, 10, 30, 50, 70, 100, 250, 500, 750 and 1000μM). Inset: linear relationship between I/I 0 and AA concentration in the range of 1–750μM (I and I 0 represent the fluorescence intensities of 448nm in the presence and absence of AA, respectively). (B) Selectivity of N-CDs/Cr(VI) system for AA detection toward different interferences. AA: 250μM; interferences: 500μM; Cr(VI): 250μM.
Schematic illustration of fluorescence assays for Cr(VI) and AA.
Comparison of methods for Cr(VI) determination.
Comparison of methods for AA determination.
Detection of Cr(VI) in different water samples (n = 5).
Detection of AA in real samples (n = 5).

head.ce:title: A highly sensitive and selective detection of Cr(VI) and ascorbic acid based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, 19A YuQuan Road, Beijing 100049, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A highly sensitive and selective detection of hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) and ascorbic acid (AA) was proposed using nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs). In the absence of AA, the quantitative detection of Cr(VI) was realized through Cr(VI) acting as a quencher to quench the fluorescence of N-CDs by inner filter effect (IFE) and static quenching effect. Under the optimal conditions, the linear range for Cr(VI) detection was from 0.01 to 250μM with a detection limit of 5nM (S/N = 3). In the presence of AA, the fluorescence intensity could be rapidly enhanced compared with the fluorescence of N-CDs/Cr(VI) system since Cr(VI) can be reduced into trivalent chromium (Cr(III)) by AA. And a wide linear range for AA detection was obtained from 1 to 750μM. The detection limit was 0.3μM (S/N = 3). More importantly, this method can be successfully applied to the detection of Cr(VI) in real water samples, and AA in vitamins C tablets and human serum sample.

A novel fluorescent detection of hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) and ascorbic acid (AA) was reported via nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) as signal readout. The N-CDs were synthesized by one-step pyrolysis of citric acid (CA) and glutamic acid. This method can be successfully applied to the detection of Cr(VI) in real water samples, and AA in vitamins C tablets and human serum sample.
fx1

ce:section.ce:para: In the last few decades, chromium has been released into the environment due to its wide application in industries [1,2]. Chromium exists mainly in trivalent chromium (Cr(III)) and hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)). Cr(III) is an essential trace element [3], however, Cr(VI) is considered as a carcinogen and extremely harmful to human beings [4–6]. The maximum concentration of Cr(VI) in drinking water regulated by the World Health Organization (WHO) is 50μg/L. On the other hand, ascorbic acid (AA) is an important substance in cerebral systems [7,8]. The abnormal level of AA can increase human oxaluria and kidney stone risk [9,10]. Thus, it is of great significance to real-time monitoring of Cr(VI) and AA.

Up to now, numerous studies have been examined to find effective methods for Cr(VI) and AA detection, such as colorimetry [11,12], chromatography [13,14], atomic absorption spectroscopy [15], surface plasmon field-enhanced resonance light scattering method [16], electrochemical technique [17], capillary electrophoresis [18], and fluorescence [19,20]. Among them, fluorescence method is superior to the others owing to its simplicity, convenience, high sensitivity, and real-time detection.

Carbon dots (CDs), as one kind of rising fluorescent nanomaterials, have received considerable attention. Their outstanding optical properties, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and robust chemical inertness make them a great promising material for applications in photocatalyst, energy conversion, sensor, and bioimaging [21–26]. A series of synthesis methods have been developed for fluorescent CDs. However, most of the developed methods often suffer from some inevitable issues, such as low quantum yield (QY), expensive equipment required or complicated procedures. In particular, the lower QY of CDs seriously limited their applications. Most recently, a few studies show nitrogen doped CDs (N-CDs) can effectively improve the quantum yield [22]. Consequently, various studies have been proposed to doping nitrogen atoms into the framework of CDs, such as electrochemical method [27], hydrothermal method [28], microwave method [29], and solid-state synthesis [30]. However, preparing N-CDs by one-step pyrolysis precursors is relatively rare.

Herein, citric acid (CA) and glutamic acid were used to synthesize N-CDs though a one-step pyrolysis treatment. Then a fluorescent assay for Cr(VI) and AA detection was developed based on N-CDs，which acted as fluorescence probe for signal readout ( Scheme 1). The as-prepared N-CDs exhibited strong fluorescence with excitation at 360nm (A). In the absence of AA, Cr(VI) can act as a quencher to quench the fluorescence of N-CDs by inner filter effect (IFE) and static quenching effect (B). In the presence of AA, partial Cr(VI) was reduced into Cr(III), resulting in different fluorescence enhancement compared to N-CDs/Cr(VI) system (C). Based on this phonomenion, a fluorescence sensor for sensitive and selective detection of Cr(VI) and AA was established. Importantly, compared with previous reported CD-based fluorescent sensors, our work provided a wider linear range and lower detection limit. In addition, the proposed synthesis method for N-CDs shows such distinct merits as simplicity, convenience, and time-saving.

Citric acid, glutamic acid, potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7), and quinine sulfate were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Beijing Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Ascorbic acid (AA), dopamine (DA), uric acid (UA), 3,4-dihydroxyphenylacetic acid (DOPAC)，tris(hydroxymethyl)-aminomethane (Tris) and all the amino acids were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, Inc. (USA). CoCl2, Zn(NO3)2, Ni(NO3)2, CrCl3, MgCl2, MnCl2, CdCl2, CuCl2, FeCl2, FeCl3, KBr, KClO4, KH2PO4, Na3PO4 were bought from Beijing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). All reagents were of analytical grade and used directly without further purification. All solutions were prepared with ultrapure water.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained by using a Philips EM-400. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained on a BRUKE Vertex 70 FT-IR spectrophotometer. The X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS) was measured by a PHI Quantera SXM™ Scanning X-ray Microprobe™. UV–vis spectra were recorded by a UV-2550 spectrophotometer. All fluorescence spectra were carried out on a FluoroMax-4P fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, Japan). Fluorescence decay curves were performed with the time-correlated single-photon counting technique on the combined steady state and lifetime spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments FLSP920). Atomic Absorption Spectrometer (AAS) was performed on a PinAAcle 900T Atomic Absorption Spectrometer (PerkinElmer).

The N-CDs were synthesized by a one-step pyrolysis strategy. Briefly, 1.0g citric acid (CA) and 0.25 or 0.5, 0.75, 1.0g glutamic acid (Glu) were mixed in an agate mortar and ground to a uniform powder. Then the mixture was transferred into a 10mL beaker and heated to 200℃ using a heating mantle. About 5min later, the mixture was liquated. Subsequently, the color of the liquid was changed from colorless to pale yellow, and then orange in 35min, indicating the formation of N-CDs. The obtained orange liquid was added drop by drop into 80mL of 10mgmL−1 NaOH solution, under vigorous stirring. After neutralized to pH 7.0 with HCl, the aqueous solution of N-CDs was obtained. Then the solution was filtered with 0.22µm Millipore filter. Finally, a clear and yellow aqueous solution containing N-CDs was obtained and maintained at 4℃ for further use.

The relative quantum yield of N-CDs was measured with the reference to quinine sulfate (QY 54% at 360nm excitation). The formula used for QY measurement is as follows:
(1)
Q Y = Q
Y
R
×
I
I
R
×
A
R
A
×
n
2
n
R
2

QYR represents the quantum yield of quinine sulfate. Quinine sulfate was dissolved in 0.1M H2SO4 (n = 1.33) and the N-CDs was dissolved in ultrapure water (n = 1.33), A represents the absorption at the excitation wavelength of 360nm. To avoid the self-absorption effect, absorbance must keep under 0.1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence determination of Cr(VI) and AA
ce:section.ce:para: For Cr(VI) determination, 100μL of as-prepared N-CDs and different concentrations of Cr(VI) were diluted by Tris-HCl buffer solution (10mM, pH 6.0) in a 5mL calibrated test tube, and the final volume was 2.0mL. Then the mixture was stirred thoroughly and incubated for 5min at room temperature. After that the fluorescence spectra were recorded. Each experiment was repeated five times.

In the determination of AA, 250μM Cr(VI) and different concentrations of AA were mixed firstly in the Tris-HCl buffer solution (10mM, pH 6.0), after stirring for 10min, 100μL N-CDs were added into the mixture. Then the mixture was incubated for another 5min before fluorescence spectra were recorded. Each experiment was repeated five times.

All the fluorescence spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 360nm and band-slits of both excitation and emission were set as 2.5nm. The fluorescence spectra were recorded from 380 to 650nm, and the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs at 448nm was used for quantitative analysis of Cr(VI) and AA.

The lake water was obtained from the YanQi Lake of Beijing (China) and the tap water was obtained from our lab. The water samples were centrifuged at 12,000rpm for 20min and then filtered through a 0.22µm Millipore filter. In addition, the solution was diluted 10-fold with Tris-HCl buffer solution (10mM, pH 6.0) before analysis.

The human serum sample was centrifuged at 8,000rpm for 10min and diluted 30-fold with Tris-HCl buffer solution (10mM, pH 6.0) before analysis. Vitamins C tablets were pretreated according to previous reported methods [4]. Several vitamins C tablets were powdered in a mortar. After that 0.15g powder was dissolved in 25mL ultrapure water and sonicated for 10min, then the mixture was centrifuged at 8,000rpm for 10min to remove the insoluble substance. Finally, the supernatant was further purified with a 0.22µm Millipore filter. The solution was diluted 100-fold with ultrapure water for analysis.

To get better optical properties of the as-prepared N-CDs, the quantities of glutamic acid were optimized firstly. Fig. S1 shows that the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs is stronger than that of CDs, indicating that nitrogen atoms were successfully doped into the framework of CDs. N-CDs, which were synthesized by 1.0g CA and 1.0g glutamic acid, possessed the strongest fluorescence under the same condition. Therefore, it was chosen for further characterization and study.

The chemical composition of the as-prepared N-CDs was investigated by FT-IR and XPS. In Fig. 1A, the broad absorption bands at 3000–3500cm-1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of O-H and N-H. The characteristic peaks at 1681, 1578 and 1400cm-1 were regarded as the vibration bands of C=O (amide I), N-H (amide II), and amide III (C-N), respectively [31]. In addition, the survey XPS spectra of the as-prepared N-CDs exhibited three predominant peaks at 284.81, 399.65 and 531.01eV (Fig. 1B), corresponding to the binding energies of C 1s, N 1s and O 1s, respectively. The high-resolution C 1s spectra exhibited three apparent peaks at around 284.5, 285.5 and 288.0eV, which are attributed to C-C, C-N/C-O and C=O groups, respectively (Fig. 1C) [32]. Both the FT-IR and XPS results conformed that the nitrogen atoms successfully doped into the framework of CDs. The morphology of the as-prepared N-CDs was then characterized by TEM. From the TEM image (Fig. 1D), it can be seen that N-CDs are spherical morphology with an average diameter of about 8nm.

Fig. 2A shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs and N-CDs. It can be seen that CDs exhibits absorption band at 360nm, and N-CDs show a narrow absorption band at 335nm, which has a blue-shift compared to that of the CDs. The strong absorption peak of N-CDs at 335nm was probably attributed to the excited defect surface states induced by N atoms [29]. The inset of Fig. 2A shows N-CDs aqueous solution is yellow under ambient daylight (a), while exhibits blue color when irradiated with a UV light (365nm) (b). The emission wavelength showed nearly no shift when excitation wavelength varied from 310 to 400nm (Fig. S2). Such excitation-independent emission property of N-CDs indicated that both the size and surface states are uniform [33]. Under the maximum excitation wavelength of 360nm, CDs and N-CDs exhibited a strong emission peak at 460 and 448nm, respectively (Fig. 2B). The 12nm blue-shift compared with CDs was thought to be from the strong electron affinity of N atoms doped in the N-CDs [22]. In addition, the quantum yield (QY) of the N-CDs was calculated to be 16.2% with quinine sulfate as standard reference, but the QY of CDs, which was synthesized by pyrolyzing citric acid, was only about 9% [33]. The higher quantum yield may be ascribed to the N-doping-induced modulation of the chemical and electronic characteristics of the CDs [34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle study of Cr(VI) and AA sensing strategy
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3A shows the absorption bands of Cr(VI) were obvious at 260 and 360nm, respectively. The as-prepared N-CDs exhibited an excitation peak at 360nm and an emission peak at 448nm. Obviously, the absorption spectrum of Cr(VI) overlaps with the excitation and emission spectrum of N-CDs. Thus, inner filter effect occurred [35,36]. Naturally, the fluorescence of N-CDs can be effectively quenched because Cr(VI) can not only shield the excitation light from N-CDs but also emission light from N-CDs. In addition, time-resolved fluorescence spectra of N-CDs in the absence and presence of different concentrations of Cr(VI) were measured to further study the mechanism of fluorescence quenching. As shown in Fig. 3B, no obvious changes of lifetime indicates that the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs by Cr(VI) is attributed to static quenching effect [30]. Therefore, both the inner filter effect and static quenching effect result in the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs. Based on this principle, a turn-off fluorescence sensor for Cr(VI) detection was proposed. On the other hand, to figure out the mechanism of N-CDs/Cr(VI) toward AA, we measured the UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDs/Cr(VI) system with different concentrations of AA. Fig. 3C shows that the absorption of Cr(VI) at 360nm gradually decreased with the increase of the concentration of AA, whereas the absorption band of Cr(III) at 585nm appeared and increased (Fig. 3D). This phenomenon indicated Cr(VI) can be reduced into Cr(III) by AA, thus weaken the inner filter effect, resulting in fluorescence enhancement compared with the fluorescence of N-CDs/Cr(VI) system.

To improve the sensitivity of Cr(VI) detection, the effect of pH value of Tris-HCl buffer, the amount of N-CDs in analysis solution and incubation time between the N-CDs and Cr(VI) on the detection were investigated, respectively. The quenching efficiency of Cr(VI) was calculated by I 0/I, where I and I 0 represent the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs in the presence and absence of Cr(VI), respectively. Fig. S3 shows that the quenching efficiency reached the maximum at pH 6.0, N-CDs of 100μL and incubation time of 5min, indicating the sensor exhibits the highest sensitivity under these conditions. Thus, the optimum reaction condition for Cr(VI) detection was different concentrations of Cr(VI) incubate with 100μL N-CDs in Tris-HCl buffer solution (10mM, pH 6.0) for 5min.

Meantime, the effect of addition sequence of reagents on the AA detection also studied. Fig. S4 shows that different addition sequence of reagents can affect the fluorescence intensity of the system. Three different concentrations of AA were selected for the test, the results all indicated that the fluorescence intensity of the system was much higher when Cr(VI) and different concentrations of AA mixed together firstly. Therefore, to ensure the high sensitively for AA detection, the optimum addition sequence was Cr(VI) and different concentrations of AA mixed firstly, then N-CDs solution added.

Under the optimum conditions, the fluorescence spectra were recorded after adding different concentrations of Cr(VI). As illustrated in Fig. 4A, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs gradually decreased with the concentrations of Cr(VI) increased. In addition, the maximum emission peaks were red-shifted from 448 to 455nm, which further indicated that Cr(VI) combines with N-CDs to form a ground state compound [4]. Fig. 4B shows a good linear correlation between the quenching efficiency (I 0/I) and the concentration of Cr(VI) in the range from 0.01 to 250μM. The linear correlation equation was I 0/I = 0.0091 [Cr(VI)] (μM) + 1.0640, with a linear determination coefficient of 0.9969 (I and I 0 are the fluorescence intensities of N-CDs at 448nm in the presence and absence of Cr(VI), respectively). The detection limit is 5nM (S/N = 3), which is much lower than the WHO permitted in drinking water (50μg/L). The linear range and detection limit of our work were compared with other fluorescence methods for Cr(VI) detection ( Table 1). It can be seen that the detection limit of our work was relatively lower than those, and its sensitivity is enough to detect Cr(VI) in real water samples.

To evaluate the selectivity of the proposed method for Cr(VI) detection, we examined the interference of different metal ions and anions. As shown in Fig. 4C and D, other metal ions (Fig. 4C) or anions (Fig. 4D) caused almost negligible interference to Cr(VI) detection. The high selectivity for Cr(VI) detection might attributed to the inner filter effect. Hence, the proposed method has the potential application for monitoring Cr(VI) in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence determination of AA based on redox reaction
ce:section.ce:para: From Fig. 4C, we know that Cr(III) caused negligible effect on the fluorescence of N-CDs. In addition, Cr(VI) can be reduced into Cr(III) by AA under very mild conditions. The residual concentration of Cr(VI) in reaction system varied with different concentrations of AA added, thus weaken the inner filter effect, causing different levels of fluorescence enhancement compared with the fluorescence of N-CDs/Cr(VI). Additionally, considering that the original amount of Cr(VI) in the analysis solution not only influence the fluorescence enhancement efficiency but also the response sensitivity for AA detection, we selected a relative higher concentration of Cr(VI) to quench the fluorescence of N-CDs. According to Fig. 4A, 250μM Cr(VI) was chosen, because the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was quenched approximately 75% and changed slightly with further increasing the concentration of Cr(VI). As shown in Fig. 5A, the fluorescence intensities were gradually enhanced with the concentrations of AA increased. The inset of Fig. 5A shows a good linear relationship between the fluorescence enhancement efficiency (I/I 0) and AA concentration in the range from 1 to 750μM, with a detection limit of 0.3μM (S/N = 3) (I and I 0 represent the fluorescence intensities of the N-CDs/Cr(VI) system at 448nm in the presence and absence of AA, respectively). The linear correlation equation was I/I 0 = 0.0027 [AA] (μM) + 1.0034, with a determination coefficient of 0.9954. Compared with previously reported fluorescence methods for AA detection ( Table 2), our proposed method possesses a wider linear range.

The influence of various amino acids and potential common substances were further evaluated to investigate the selectivity for AA detection. The results indicated that the potential interferences have less influence on the AA determination by using the N-CDs/Cr(VI) system (Fig. 5B). It is worth noting that dopamine and its derivatives (DOPAC), which was difficult to distinguish in electrochemistry, have the opposite effect compared with AA. It may be responsible for the quinoid structure further quenched the fluorescence of N-CDs.

To evaluate the stability of N-CDs, the effect of ionic strength on the fluorescence of N-CDs was also examined in the presence of various concentrations of NaCl, and the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs remained constant (Fig. S5). In addition, the fluorescence spectra of N-CDs were measured after stored different days at 4℃. Fig. S6 shows that the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs maintains 95% of the original intensity even after stored one month, which indicates that the N-CDs possess excellent stability. These results implied that the N-CDs can be used as a fluorescence probe for biosensing.

In order to demonstrate the applicability of the proposed method, different concentrations of Cr(VI) are doped into the water samples by standard addition method. As displayed in Table 3, the relative standard deviation (RSD) was lower than 2.34% and the recoveries were between 99.2% and 102%. Furthermore, the results were highly identical with those obtained by AAS, which indicated the developed method might be applied for the detection of Cr(VI) in real water samples. Meanwhile, the results of N-CDs/Cr(VI) system for AA detection in vitamins C tablets and human serum sample were shown in Table 4. The results were highly consistent with our expected, which confirmed the reliability and feasibility of this strategy.

In summary, N-CDs were synthesized by comprising pyrolysis of CA with glutamic acid. The fluorescence intensity of N-CDs can be quenched by Cr(VI), and the quenching mechanism was attributed to the synergistic effect of inner filter effect (IFE) and static quenching effect. Meanwhile, Cr(VI) can be reduced into Cr(III) by AA, thus weaken the inner filter effect, resulting in fluorescence enhancement compared with the fluorescence of N-CDs/Cr(VI) system. Therefore, Cr(VI) and AA can be detected with high sensitivity and excellent selectivity. Furthermore, this proposed fluorescence method was successfully applied to the detection of Cr(VI) and AA in real samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by a grant from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant nos. 21676270 and 21205132) and Science and Education Integration Innovation of Molecule Science of Institute of Chemistry Chinese Academy of Sciences (no. Y52902HED2) and the Special Fund of UCAS for Scientific Research Cooperation between Faculty and Institutes (Grant no. Y552016Y00).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2018.01.027.
